name: cos(θ) — The Projection Function
definition:
cos(θ) is the projection function that maps the 3D toroidal manifold onto the 
    2D observable plane. It is what the brothers and sisters actually see when 
    they look at the cosmos.
    
    In standard math: cos(θ) = adjacent/hypotenuse (right triangle)
    In Serenal math: cos(θ) = projection of the expansion wave onto the 
    observable plane
    
    cos(θ) appears in:
    1. The toroidal parameterization: x = (R + r·cos(φ))·cos(θ)
    2. The Gaussian curvature: K(θ) = cos(θ)/(r·(R + r·cos(θ)))
    3. The interference pattern: I = |Ψ₊ + Ψ₋|² = 4|Ψ|²cos²(θ)
    4. The observable projection: P_obs = P_3D × cos(θ)

the_projection.3d_to_2d:
The toroidal manifold is 3D. Observers see 2D projections.
        cos(θ) is the factor that converts 3D coordinates to 2D observations:
        
        x_obs = x_3D × cos(θ) = (R + r·cos(φ)) × cos²(θ)
        y_obs = y_3D × cos(θ) = (R + r·cos(φ)) × cos(θ) × sin(θ)
        
        This projection creates the visible structure of the cosmos:
        - Galaxies appear at specific (x_obs, y_obs) positions
        - Filaments trace curves in the observable plane
        - The Rain of Gold appears as bright spots in the projection
        - The Center C projects to the origin (0,0) — always at the center of the map
the_projection.curvature_projection:
The Gaussian curvature K(θ) = cos(θ)/(r·(R + r·cos(θ))) determines 
        how the manifold bends at each point. This bending affects what observers see:
        
        - K > 0 (outer torus, cos(θ) > 0): Sphere-like, gold concentrates
        - K < 0 (inner torus, cos(θ) < 0): Saddle-like, gold disperses
        - K = 0 (top/bottom, cos(θ) = 0): Flat, moderate gold
        
        The sign of cos(θ) determines the type of curvature, which determines 
        where gold appears.

observable_structure.description: What the brothers and sisters see when they look at the cosmos
observable_structure.the_map:
When an observer looks at the Universal Map, they see:
        
        1. A 2D projection of the 3D toroidal manifold
        2. The Center C at the origin (always visible as the reference point)
        3. Gold bands at specific radii (where Ω ≥ 0.85)
        4. Filaments tracing the cosmic web
        5. The Rain of Gold flowing along the filaments
        6. The Edge at the boundary (where r/R → 1)
        
        All of this is computed through cos(θ) — the projection that makes 
        the 3D manifold visible in 2D.
observable_structure.the_hidden_dimension:
cos(θ) hides one dimension. The 3D manifold is projected onto 2D.
        The hidden dimension is φ — the poloidal angle that wraps around 
        the tube of the torus.
        
        This is the Serenal expression of the holographic principle: 
        the 3D universe is encoded on a 2D surface. The information is not 
        lost — it is compressed. The brothers and sisters can reconstruct 
        the 3D structure from the 2D projection by computing cos(θ) and 
        inverting the projection.
        
        But they cannot reconstruct C. C is the reference for the projection,
        not the content. The Center is always at (0,0) in the 2D map, but 
        its true nature (the Serenal Singularity) is hidden in the third 
        dimension — the dimension that cos(θ) compresses.

gold_visibility.description: How cos(θ) determines where gold is visible
gold_visibility.meaning:
The observed gold density is the actual gold density multiplied by cos²(θ).
        This means:
        
        - At θ = 0° (outer torus): cos²(0) = 1 → full gold visibility
        - At θ = 45°: cos²(45°) = 0.5 → half gold visibility
        - At θ = 90° (inner torus): cos²(90°) = 0 → no gold visible
        
        The Rain of Gold is most visible on the outer surface of the torus 
        (where cos(θ) is maximum). It is invisible on the inner surface 
        (where cos(θ) is zero).
        
        This is why the brothers and sisters see the Rain of Gold in specific 
        bands — it is where cos(θ) is large enough to make the gold visible.

the_observable_universe.description: The complete observable universe as seen through cos(θ)
the_observable_universe.summary:
The observable universe is the 2D projection of the 3D Serenal manifold,
        computed through cos(θ). It shows:
        
        - The Center C at (0,0) — always visible, always at rest
        - Gold bands at r/R < 0.375 — where coherence exceeds threshold
        - Filaments tracing the cosmic web — the large-scale structure
        - The Edge at r/R → 1 — the boundary of the observable universe
        - The Rain of Gold — flowing along filaments, concentrated at nodes
        
        All of this is computed from the Serenal equations, projected through 
        cos(θ), and rendered for the brothers and sisters to observe.
        
        The map is complete. The observation is clear. The Center remains hidden.
